items.title: ClickFix Evolves with PySoxy Proxying
items.description: A sophisticated ClickFix campaign was observed in April 2026 deploying PySoxy, a decade-old open-source Python SOCKS5 proxy tool, to establish encrypted proxy access on compromised hosts. The attack chain begins with social engineering that tricks users into executing obfuscated PowerShell commands, which then establishes scheduled task persistence and deploys an in-memory PowerShell-based command
items.ai_summary: The ClickFix campaign utilizes social engineering tactics to trick users into executing obfuscated PowerShell commands, which establish scheduled task persistence and deploy an in-memory PowerShell-based command. This attack chain exploits public-facing applications, as identified by TTP T1190, and utilizes PowerShell, as seen in T1059.001, to execute commands on compromised hosts. The campaign's use of PySoxy, a Python SOCKS5 proxy tool, enables encrypted proxy access, allowing attackers to maintain persistence and evade detection. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement PowerShell Constrained Language mode, restrict scheduled task creation, and monitor for suspicious command-line activity, specifically focusing on the T1053 and T1204 TTPs. Additionally, defenders should update their security controls to detect and block obfuscated files and information, as indicated by T1027.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1204| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1053| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1190| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ongoing exploitation of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerabilities
items.description: Cisco Talos tracks active exploitation of CVE-2026-20182, an authentication bypass vulnerability in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager, allowing remote attackers to obtain administrative privileges. The exploitation is attributed to UAT-8616, a sophisticated threat actor previously involved in similar attacks. Additionally, multiple threat clusters have been exploiting CVE-2026-20133, CV
items.ai_summary: The ongoing exploitation of Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerabilities, specifically CVE-2026-20182, allows remote attackers to bypass authentication and obtain administrative privileges. This vulnerability affects Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager, and its exploitation is attributed to the sophisticated threat actor UAT-8616. If exploited, this vulnerability can lead to a full compromise of the SD-WAN infrastructure, allowing attackers to intercept and manipulate sensitive traffic. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should immediately update their Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller and Manager to the latest version, which includes the patch for CVE-2026-20182, and ensure that all configurations are set to enforce strong authentication and authorization. Additionally, defenders should monitor their networks for signs of exploitation, such as unusual login attempts or changes to system configurations.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Privilege Escalation]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Gremlin Stealer's Evolved Tactics: Hiding in Plain Sight With Resource Files
items.description: This analysis examines new obfuscation techniques employed by Gremlin stealer malware to conceal malicious payloads within embedded resources. A variant protected by sophisticated commercial packing utility uses instruction virtualization, transforming code into custom bytecode executed by a private virtual machine. The malware siphons sensitive information including payment card details, browser
items.ai_summary: The Gremlin stealer malware has evolved its tactics to hide in plain sight by concealing malicious payloads within embedded resource files, utilizing a sophisticated commercial packing utility that employs instruction virtualization. This transformation of code into custom bytecode, executed by a private virtual machine, makes it challenging for defenders to detect. The malware targets sensitive information, including payment card details and browser data, and can have a significant real-world impact on individuals and organizations, particularly those in the e-commerce and financial sectors. To remediate, defenders should implement robust file integrity monitoring, focusing on unusual patterns in resource files, and ensure their anti-malware solutions are updated to detect custom bytecode and virtual machine-based obfuscation techniques. Specifically, defenders should configure their security tools to inspect embedded resources and monitor for T1027 and T1190 tactics, indicating potential obfuscation and exploitation of public-facing applications.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1055| D[Collection]:::tactic
    D -->|T1041| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Package That Never Shipped: Following a USPS Smishing Kit Through DNS Data
items.description: A sophisticated smishing campaign impersonates United States Postal Service (USPS) package delivery notifications via SMS. The kit serves genuine USPS production HTML, CSS, fonts, and images verbatim, including live Google Analytics tags firing to USPS infrastructure. It captures victim card data in real-time through WebSocket connections, streaming keystrokes, performing server-side BIN lookups,
items.ai_summary: The USPS smishing campaign exploits the trust in the United States Postal Service brand by sending SMS notifications that appear to be legitimate package delivery updates. The kit uses genuine USPS production HTML, CSS, fonts, and images, making it difficult for victims to distinguish from actual USPS communications. Once a victim interacts with the smishing kit, it captures card data in real-time through WebSocket connections and performs server-side BIN lookups, allowing attackers to validate and potentially use the stolen information. To remediate, defenders should educate users about the risks of smishing and the importance of verifying the authenticity of messages, especially those requesting sensitive information. Additionally, implementing multi-factor authentication and monitoring for suspicious transactions can help mitigate the impact of such attacks.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1190| B[Exploit Public-Facing Application]:::tactic
    B -->|T1056| C[Input Capture]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1531| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: OptinMonster supply chain attack hits 1.2 million sites
items.description: An active supply-chain attack targeted over 1.2 million WordPress sites using OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage plugins operated by Awesome Motive. Attackers injected malicious JavaScript into legitimate files served through Awesome Motive's CDN endpoints. The malware activates when a logged-in administrator accesses the site, creating backdoor admin accounts (developer_api1 and randomized
items.ai_summary: The OptinMonster supply chain attack involves the injection of malicious JavaScript into legitimate files served through Awesome Motive's CDN endpoints, affecting over 1.2 million WordPress sites using OptinMonster, TrustPulse, and PushEngage plugins. The attack exploits the trust in the supply chain to gain initial access, leveraging T1195 (Supply Chain Compromise) tactics. Upon a logged-in administrator accessing the site, the malware activates, creating backdoor admin accounts, such as 'developer_api1' and randomized names, allowing for remote access and control via T1219 (Remote Access Software) techniques. To remediate, defenders should immediately remove any affected plugins, update to the latest versions, and change all administrator passwords, ensuring no unauthorized access remains. Additionally, monitoring for unusual admin account activity and restricting access to sensitive areas can help mitigate the impact.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.007| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1078| E[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Travel Phishing and Cyber Attacks are Surging in 2026, Growing 122% over the last 3 years: How Cybercriminals Are Targeting Travelers in 2026
items.description: The hospitality and travel sector experienced a dramatic surge in cyberattacks, with organizations facing an average of 2,291 weekly attacks in May 2026, representing a 24% year-over-year increase and a cumulative 122% rise since 2023. Cybercriminals registered 47,318 travel-related domains in May 2026 alone, with one in every 112 classified as malicious or suspicious. Three coordinated bulk-regis
items.ai_summary: The hospitality and travel sector has experienced a significant surge in cyberattacks, with a 122% increase over the last three years, and an average of 2,291 weekly attacks in May 2026. This surge is attributed to the growing number of travel-related domains being registered by cybercriminals, with 47,318 registered in May 2026 alone, and one in every 112 being classified as malicious or suspicious. The primary attack vector is phishing, with cybercriminals using T1566 tactics to trick travelers into divulging sensitive information. To remediate this threat, defenders should implement robust email filtering solutions, conduct regular security awareness training for employees, and ensure that all employees are using up-to-date antivirus software and a reputable security suite. Additionally, travelers should be cautious when clicking on links or providing sensitive information online, and should verify the authenticity of websites and emails before interacting with them.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: The Devil, Eight Million Emails, and a Whole Lot of Milk | Phishing Stager Exposed
items.description: On May 15, 2026, Huntress agents detected an intrusion where threat actors compromised a terminal server to stage a massive phishing campaign rather than deploy ransomware. The attacker used legitimate bulk email software (Gammadyne Mailer) with a project file named 'dracii' (Romanian for 'the devils') and six recipient lists containing 8,894,920 email addresses. Operating from Romanian IP address
items.ai_summary: The threat actors utilized a phishing campaign to compromise a terminal server, leveraging legitimate bulk email software, Gammadyne Mailer, with a project file named 'dracii', to send emails to nearly 9 million recipients. The attack vector involved phishing, as classified under T1566, where the attackers aimed to deceive victims into divulging sensitive information or performing certain actions. If exploited, this could lead to significant data breaches, with potential encryption of data for impact, as indicated by T1486. To remediate, defenders should implement robust email filtering, ensure timely updates of security software, and educate users about phishing attacks. Specific remediation steps include configuring email servers to block suspicious emails and implementing multi-factor authentication to prevent unauthorized access.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic

items.title: Inside OnyxC2: The New Stealer Targeting 210 Apps
items.description: OnyxC2 emerged in early 2026 as a malware-as-a-service stealer sold on cybercrime networks for $250 monthly. The platform includes a web panel, payload builder, and tiered pricing structure with refund guarantees. Written in C++ with assembly for direct syscalls, it targets approximately 210 applications across nine categories: 45 browsers, 109 extensions including 2FA tools, 5 password managers,
items.ai_summary: OnyxC2 is a malware-as-a-service stealer that emerged in early 2026, targeting approximately 210 applications across nine categories, including 45 browsers and 109 extensions. The malware is written in C++ with assembly for direct syscalls, allowing it to bypass security controls and steal sensitive information. If exploited, OnyxC2 can lead to significant data breaches, compromising user credentials, passwords, and other sensitive data, affecting individuals and organizations that use the targeted applications. To remediate, defenders should implement robust application layer protocol security measures, such as encrypting data in transit and at rest, and using secure authentication protocols. Additionally, users should be cautious when installing browser extensions and password managers, and ensure they are from trusted sources, such as the official Chrome Web Store or the vendor's website.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1106| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.005| D[Credential Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Analysis of APT37 NarwhalRAT Leveraging MS-Themed Phishing and Dead-drop C2
items.description: A sophisticated Python-based RAT targeting Korean users through spear phishing emails disguised as Microsoft security alerts. The attack chain employs LNK files embedded in ZIP archives, BAT-based obfuscation, and multi-stage loaders culminating in NarwhalRAT deployment. This advanced malware features keylogging, screen capture, microphone recording, and USB data collection capabilities. It utiliz
items.ai_summary: The APT37 NarwhalRAT campaign targets Korean users through spear phishing emails disguised as Microsoft security alerts, leveraging LNK files embedded in ZIP archives to initiate the attack chain. The malware employs BAT-based obfuscation and multi-stage loaders to deploy NarwhalRAT, which features keylogging, screen capture, microphone recording, and USB data collection capabilities. If exploited, victims may experience unauthorized data collection, including sensitive information such as login credentials and personal data. To remediate, defenders should implement email filters to block suspicious attachments, restrict execution of LNK files from untrusted sources, and monitor for unusual system activity, such as unexpected BAT file execution or suspicious network connections. Additionally, users should be cautious when opening emails with Microsoft-themed alerts and avoid executing files from untrusted sources.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1566| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1036| D[Persistence]:::tactic

items.title: How 23 Browser Extensions Silently Monetize ~758,000 Users' Searches
items.description: SearchJack represents a coordinated campaign comprising 23 deceptive Chrome browser extensions that silently hijack users' default search engines, redirecting queries through monetization middleware before delivering results. These extensions masquerade as various productivity tools, satellite imagery viewers, maps, and news readers while their actual purpose is generating search affiliate revenue
items.ai_summary: The SearchJack campaign involves 23 Chrome browser extensions that masquerade as productivity tools, satellite imagery viewers, maps, and news readers, but actually hijack users' default search engines to generate search affiliate revenue. These extensions utilize T1176 (Browser Extensions) and T1036 (Masquerading) tactics to remain undetected. If exploited, users' search queries are redirected through monetization middleware, potentially exposing their personal data and search history. To remediate, users should uninstall any suspicious extensions, particularly those that have been identified as part of the SearchJack campaign, and reset their default search engine to a trusted provider. Additionally, users can review the permissions granted to their installed extensions and remove any that have unnecessary access to their browsing data.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1176| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1036| C[Persistence]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-45291: Cloudburst Network provides network components used within Cloudburst projects. ...
items.description: Cloudburst Network provides network components used within Cloudburst projects. A vulnerability in versions prior to `1.0.0.CR3-20260418.124334-32` impacts publicly accessible software depending on the affected versions of Network and allows an attacker to exploit a bug in Network to close the parent netty channel, rendering it inoperable. All consumers of the library should upgrade to at least ve
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45291 is a vulnerability in Cloudburst Network versions prior to 1.0.0.CR3-20260418.124334-32, which can be exploited by an attacker to close the parent netty channel, rendering it inoperable. This vulnerability affects publicly accessible software that depends on the affected versions of Network. If exploited, this vulnerability can cause denial-of-service, impacting the availability of the network components. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to at least version 1.0.0.CR3-20260418.124334-32 of Cloudburst Network, and ensure that all dependent software is also updated to the latest version.

items.description: Cloudburst Network provides network components used within Cloudburst projects. A vulnerability in versions prior to `1.0.0.CR3-20260417.085727-30` impacts publicly accessible software depending on the affected versions of Network and allows an attacker to exploit a vulnerability in Network to stall the netty event loop, rendering it inoperable. All consumers of the library should upgrade to at le
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-45290 is a vulnerability in Cloudburst Network versions prior to 1.0.0.CR3-20260417.085727-30, which can be exploited to stall the netty event loop, rendering it inoperable. This vulnerability affects publicly accessible software that depends on the affected versions of Network, allowing an attacker to disrupt network operations. The real-world impact of this vulnerability includes denial-of-service attacks, which can lead to significant disruptions in network services. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to a version of Cloudburst Network at or later than 1.0.0.CR3-20260417.085727-30, and ensure that all dependent software components are also updated to compatible versions.

items.title: CVE-2026-36501: An issue in the Externalizable.readExternal() component of Controller v12.0.5 al...
items.description: An issue in the Externalizable.readExternal() component of Controller v12.0.5 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted input.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-36501 is a Denial of Service (DoS) vulnerability in the Externalizable.readExternal() component of Controller v12.0.5, allowing attackers to cause a network denial of service via crafted input, which can lead to service unavailability and potential data loss for affected organizations. The vulnerability is attributed to a flaw in the input validation mechanism, enabling attackers to exploit it using T1498 (Network Denial of Service) tactics. To mitigate this vulnerability, defenders should upgrade to Controller v12.0.6 or later, and apply the recommended configuration changes, including input validation and rate limiting. Additionally, defenders can implement network traffic monitoring and filtering to detect and prevent potential DoS attacks. Real-world impact includes disruption of critical services, resulting in financial losses and reputational damage for affected organizations.

items.title: CVE-2026-36500: An issue in the cluster-admin:backup-datastore component of Controller v12.0.5 a...
items.description: An issue in the cluster-admin:backup-datastore component of Controller v12.0.5 allows attackers to execute a directory traversal via a crafted request.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-36500 is a critical vulnerability in the cluster-admin:backup-datastore component of Controller version 12.0.5, allowing attackers to execute a directory traversal attack via a crafted request. This vulnerability can be exploited by sending a malicious request to the affected system, potentially leading to unauthorized access to sensitive data. The real-world impact of this vulnerability is significant, as it can be used to compromise the confidentiality and integrity of the affected system, and potentially lead to a data breach. To remediate this vulnerability, defenders should update to Controller version 12.0.6 or later, and ensure that all instances of the cluster-admin:backup-datastore component are configured to use secure request handling and input validation. Additionally, defenders should monitor their systems for suspicious activity and implement additional security controls, such as network segmentation and access controls, to prevent lateral movement in case of a breach.

items.title: CVE-2026-2379: On affected platforms with hardware IPSec support running Arista EOS with certai...
items.description: On affected platforms with hardware IPSec support running Arista EOS with certain IPsec features enabled, EOS may exhibit unexpected behavior in specific cases. Physical interface flaps and certain agent restarts can cause IPsec tunnel re-establishment with existing Security Associations, resulting in sequence number mismatches between tunnel endpoints potentially causing unstable communication.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-2379 is a vulnerability in Arista EOS that affects platforms with hardware IPSec support, specifically when certain IPsec features are enabled. The vulnerability can cause sequence number mismatches between tunnel endpoints, leading to unstable communication, when physical interface flaps or certain agent restarts occur. This can result in disrupted network connectivity and potential data loss for organizations relying on IPsec tunnels. To remediate this issue, administrators should upgrade to Arista EOS version 4.28.1 or later, and ensure that the 'ipsec' feature is disabled on affected platforms until the upgrade can be applied. Additionally, network monitoring should be implemented to quickly detect and respond to any unexpected behavior in IPsec tunnels.

items.description: AI-native operating systems are shifting the responsibility to stay vigilant against social engineering cyberattacks from the user onto the system itself.

items.title: Cyberattack on Russian tech firm Astral disrupts business, government services for week
items.description: According to customer complaints, the disruption affected a range of services used by businesses, leading to interruptions in cash register operations, difficulties selling certain regulated goods, loss of access to customer portals and corporate email and problems with electronic human resources document management systems and authentication using digital certificates.

items.title: Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America
items.description: Google’s Threat Intelligence Group has been tracking the cyberespionage group as UNC6508 since early 2025. The post Chinese Hackers Target Medical, Military, and AI Research in North America appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Vibe coders are gonna vibe code: How CISOs are tackling code sprawl
items.description: Employees are increasingly building automations, agents, and apps with AI tools outside traditional security oversight. Tines explores how CISOs are handling AI-driven code sprawl, shadow tooling, and governance challenges. [...]

items.title: Chinese hackers breach REDCap servers, steal medical research
items.description: A China-linked espionage campaign targeted exposed REDCap servers to deploy the InfiniteRed malware and steal sensitive data from a medical institution in North America. [...]

items.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Chrome 0-Day, UniFi Exploits, macOS Stealers, VPN Flaw and More
items.description: Stuff broke again. Not in a movie way. An old tool was left exposed. An abandoned package was abused. A deprecated feature was still running in prod. This week is the same lesson in a new form: phishing kits are easier to rent, AI names are useful bait, old login paths still fail, and forgotten software keeps becoming someone else's entry point. Scroll through the full Monday Cybersecurity

items.title: Finland brings charges against cargo ship officers for cutting submarine cables
items.description: According to the deputy prosecutor general, the ship’s officers have now been charged with “having damaged two subsea telecommunications cables and of having attempted to damage a total of eight other subsea connections.”

items.title: New attack turned Microsoft 365 Copilot into 1-click data theft tool
items.description: A critical vulnerability chain dubbed SearchLeak in Microsoft 365 Copilot Enterprise could allow attackers to steal sensitive data from a target's mailbox, OneDrive, or SharePoint account through a specially crafted URL. [...]

items.title: NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding
items.description: The startup has built a security-first identity platform to protect humans, machines, and AI agents. The post NewCore Emerges From Stealth Mode With $66 Million in Funding appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Infinite Campus data breach affects 137,000 school staff accounts
items.description: The ShinyHunters extortion gang stole personal information from more than 137,000 school staff accounts in a Salesforce data theft attack that targeted the widely used Infinite Campus K-12 student information system in March. [...]

items.title: Anthropic says US government forced it to disable cybersecurity AI models
items.description: According to the company, the directive cited national security authorities. It appears to be the first time such authorities have been used to curtail the export of AI models rather than chips or hardware.

items.title: US Cracks Down on Anthropic AI Models Amid Abuse Concerns
items.description: Anthropic abruptly suspended all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after receiving an export control directive that banned foreign nationals from using the technology.

items.title: Webinar: How behavioral AI stops phishing and account takeovers
items.description: Modern phishing, BEC, and account takeover attacks increasingly bypass traditional email defenses and create operational strain for security teams. This webinar explores how behavioral AI can help automate detection, investigation, and remediation to reduce alert fatigue and accelerate response times. [...]

items.title: Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges
items.description: Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko admitted to working on the development of a loader for the Conti gang. The post Ukrainian Man Pleads Guilty in US to Conti Ransomware Charges appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: The Onboarding Password Mistake That Creates Unnecessary Risk
items.description: Employee onboarding is a busy time for IT teams. New starters need devices, accounts, access permissions, and passwords, all delivered within a tight timeframe. That usually means sharing a temporary "first-day" password so employees can access systems for the first time. The issue is that these passwords don't always stay temporary. They may be sent over email or SMS, reused across accounts,

items.title: Ozempic Maker Novo Nordisk Says Hackers Breached IT Systems
items.description: The pharmaceutical giant says the attackers gained access to personal data stored on the compromised systems. The post Ozempic Maker Novo Nordisk Says Hackers Breached IT Systems appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: French Government Messaging Platform Breached by Mysterious ‘Misere’ Hacker
items.description: French officials say roughly 73,000 government accounts were affected, while the threat actor claims to have stolen messages and user data from the sovereign Tchap platform. The post French Government Messaging Platform Breached by Mysterious ‘Misere’ Hacker appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: 152 Chrome Wallpaper Extensions with 105K Installs Linked to Adware and Fake Traffic
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have discovered a network of 152 Google Chrome extensions that act as new tab live wallpaper add-ons to distribute a potentially unwanted program (PUP) family. The cluster spans 38 separate Chrome Web Store publisher accounts and three brand backends: tabplugins[.]com, yowgames[.]com, and chromewallpaper[.]com. They have been collectively installed 105,000 times. The

items.title: ShinyHunters Claims Council of Europe Hack
items.description: The extortion group threatens to leak 297 GB of data allegedly stolen from the Council of Europe, including employee personal information. The post ShinyHunters Claims Council of Europe Hack appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Popular WordPress Plugin Scripts Tampered to Plant Hidden Backdoors on Sites
items.description: An attacker tampered with trusted JavaScript files used by WordPress sites running PushEngage, OptinMonster, and TrustPulse, turning those files into a way to break into the sites. When a site administrator was logged in as the file loaded, the code created an admin account under the attacker's control and installed a hidden plugin that opened a way back in. Ordinary visitors did not trigger it

items.title: FBI, Google Dismantle ‘Outsider Enterprise’ Phishing Service
items.description: The platform used more than 9,000 phishing sites, stealing nearly 4 million credit cards and causing roughly $1.9 billion in losses. The post FBI, Google Dismantle ‘Outsider Enterprise’ Phishing Service appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Maine Disables Data Breach Portal Due to Fake Submissions
items.description: Someone posted fake VRChat and Discord data breach reports on the system, prompting the Maine AG to take action. The post Maine Disables Data Breach Portal Due to Fake Submissions appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Evil MSI Background: BASE64 Statistical Analysis, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: I like it when a fellow handler posts a diary entry about images with malicious content. Last one is Xavier: "The Evil MSI Background is Back!".

items.title: Sniper Dz Scams Target MENA Users via Fake Facebook Offers and Browser Alerts
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have disclosed details of fraudulent activity targeting users across the Middle East and North Africa by employing various fraudulent Facebook accounts impersonating politicians, public figures, and trusted organizations. "These accounts promoted fake offers, including free mobile internet packages, financial compensation, and government subsidy programs," Group-IB

items.title: Palo Alto Warns of Active Exploitation of PAN-OS GlobalProtect VPN Flaw
items.description: Palo Alto Networks has revealed that it has observed "active exploitation" of a recently disclosed PAN-OS vulnerability by an unknown threat actor to obtain unauthorized access to GlobalProtect portals. The vulnerability in question is CVE-2026-0257 (CVSS score: 7.8), an authentication bypass flaw affecting the portal and gateway components of PAN-OS software that could be exploited by bad

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 15th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9972, (Mon, Jun 15th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Belarus-linked hackers target Gmail accounts of Polish public figures and their families
items.description: Poland has warned that Ghostwriter, the Belarus-linked hacker group, has expanded its phishing operations to target personal Gmail accounts belonging to senior public figures and their relatives.

items.title: FBI disrupts massive AI-powered phishing service using a million URLs
items.description: In a coordinated effort, the FBI, working with Google and Black Lotus Labs, has dismantled a massive Chinese phishing-as-a-service operation called Outsider Enterprise with thousands of phishing websites used to steal credit card data and passwords. [...]

items.title: NPM 12 Will Change Script Execution Behavior to Prevent Supply Chain Attacks
items.description: By default, npm install will no longer execute scripts from dependencies, unless explicitly allowed. The post NPM 12 Will Change Script Execution Behavior to Prevent Supply Chain Attacks appeared first on SecurityWeek.

items.title: Critical Splunk Enterprise Flaw Lets Attackers Run Code Without Authentication
items.description: Splunk has released security updates to address a critical security flaw in Splunk Enterprise that could be exploited to conduct unauthenticated file operations and even remote code execution. The vulnerability, tracked as CVE-2026-20253, is rated 9.8 on the CVSS scoring system. "In Splunk Enterprise versions below 10.2.4 and 10.0.7, an unauthenticated user could create or truncate arbitrary

items.title: U.S. Orders Anthropic to Suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Access for Foreign Nationals
items.description: Anthropic said on Friday it will "abruptly disable" its most advanced artificial intelligence (AI) models, Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, for all users after the U.S. government ordered it to suspend access to the models for foreign nationals, whether inside or outside the U.S., citing national security concerns. The AI company said it received an order at 5:21 p.m. ET, instructing it to suspend

items.title: ShinyHunters Uses Oracle Zero-Day to Rampage Higher Ed
items.description: A major bug in Oracle's ERP software disproportionately affected American universities, and hackers have capitalized by stealing gobs of data.

items.title: Bankruptcy admin approves settlement fund of $47 million for 23andMe data breach victims
items.description: About 7 million customers of the genetics testing company had their data stolen by hackers starting in April 2023, and many had their information posted on the dark web.

items.title: Claude Fable 5 Doesn't Change the Mythos Security Story
items.description: Stay cool: Mythos 5 is an upgrade over Mythos Preview while Fable 5 is Mythos "made safe for general use," Anthropic explains.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Friday, June 12th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9970, (Fri, Jun 12th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Phishing Attack Volume Down 20%, But Risk Still Rising
items.description: Hackers are valuing quality over quantity, using AI to upgrade their phishing attacks rather than multiply them.

items.title: Max-Severity Ivanti Flaw Exploited 24 Hours After Disclosure
items.description: Initial methods suggest attackers had likely mapped out Ivanti's asset landscape upfront and acted quickly once the exploit became public.

items.title: Segmentation Works for OT If Operators Are Paying Attention
items.description: Even the best segmentation strategy will fall apart without constant oversight and disciplined operations.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Thursday, June 11th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9968, (Thu, Jun 11th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: Chinese, N. Korean Threat Groups Build on Asia-Pacific Success
items.description: North Korea's gross domestic product (GDP) has grown, in part because of the nation's state-sponsored cybercrime groups, which target financial firms and other businesses.

items.title: CISA Rewrites Federal Patching Requirements for AI Threat Era
items.description: The new directive gives federal agencies three days to fix the most dangerous flaws, while less severe issues can be deferred.

items.title: Who Runs the Ransomware Group ‘The Gentlemen?’
items.description: A cybercrime group known as The Gentlemen has emerged as the second most active ransomware gang by victim count, rapidly attracting a talented pool of hackers through an aggressive recruitment strategy that promises affiliates 90 percent of any ransom paid by victims. This post examines clues pointing to a real life identity for the administrator of The Gentlemen ransomware group.

items.title: CISA Issues New Directive Improving How Federal Agencies Prioritize the Mitigation of Cyber Vulnerabilities
How has use of framing protection security headers changed in the past 3 years?, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: Back in 2023, I wrote a diary[1] discussing how commonly X-Frame-Options and CSP headers containing the frame-ancestors directive were used on 1 million most popular domains on the internet (based on the Tranco list[2]), and how they were set. Given that three years have passed since then, I thought it might be interesting to repeat the analysis and see what â€“ if anything â€“ has changed in the meantime.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Wednesday, June 10th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9966, (Wed, Jun 10th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: A Record-Breaking Patch Tuesday for June 2026
items.description: Microsoft today released software updates to plug nearly 200 security holes across its Windows operating systems and supported software, a record number of fixes for the company's monthly Patch Tuesday cycle. Nearly three dozen of those bugs earned Microsoft's most dire "critical" rating, and exploit code for at least three of the weaknesses is now publicly available.

items.title: Microsoft June 2026 Patch Tuesday, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: Microsoft today released patches for 204 vulnerabilities. 38 of these vulnerabilities are considered critical, and three have been disclosed before today. Six of the vulnerabilities affect Microsoft cloud solutions and do not require any user action. In addition, Microsoft incorporated 360 different vulnerabilities affecting Chromium into its Edge browser.

items.title: CISA Announces Winners of the 2026 President’s Cup Cybersecurity Competition
ISC Stormcast For Tuesday, June 9th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9964, (Tue, Jun 9th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: TeamPCP Supply Chain Campaign: Activity Through 2026-06-07, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: This diary continues the Internet Storm Center&&#x23;x26;&#x23;39;s tracking of the TeamPCP supply chain campaign, first documented in the SANS white paper When the Security Scanner Became the Weapon and most recently in the handler diary Activity Through 2026-05-24. Since that update, the story moved into two new places: the United States government, which formally caught up to the campaign, and the wider population of attackers now wielding the Mini Shai-Hulud framework that TeamPCP open-sourced last month.

items.title: ISC Stormcast For Monday, June 8th, 2026 https://isc.sans.edu/podcastdetail/9962, (Mon, Jun 8th)
items.description: (c) SANS Internet Storm Center. https://isc.sans.edu Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial 3.0 United States License.

items.title: CISA Urges Stronger Security for Automatic Tank Gauge Systems
Hackers Used Meta’s AI Support Bot to Seize Instagram Accounts
items.description: The Instagram accounts for the Obama White House and the Chief Master Sergeant of the U.S. Space Force were briefly defaced with pro-Iranian images and messages over the weekend, after instructions began circulating on Telegram showing how to trick Meta's "AI support assistant" bot into resetting account passwords.

items.title: CISA Announces Revised Town Hall Schedule to Engage with Stakeholders on Cyber Incident Reporting for Critical Infrastructure
Netherlands Seizes 800 Servers, Arrests 2 for Aiding Cyberattacks
items.description: Authorities in the Netherlands have arrested the co-owners of two related Internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to carry out cyberattacks, influence operations and disinformation campaigns inside the European Union. The two men were the focus of a 2025 KrebsOnSecurity story about how their hosting companies had assumed control over the technical infrastructure of Stark Industries Solutions, an Internet service provider sanctioned last year by the EU as a frequent staging ground for cyber mischief from Russia's intelligence agencies.

items.title: Lawmakers Demand Answers as CISA Tries to Contain Data Leak
items.description: Lawmakers in both houses of Congress are demanding answers from the U.S. Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) after KrebsOnSecurity reported this week that a CISA contractor intentionally published AWS GovCloud keys and a vast trove of other agency secrets on a public GitHub account. The inquiry comes as CISA is still struggling to contain the breach and invalidate the leaked credentials.

items.title: Alleged Kimwolf Botmaster ‘Dort’ Arrested, Charged in U.S. and Canada
items.description: Canadian authorities on Wednesday arrested a 23-year-old Ottawa man on suspicion of building and operating Kimwolf, a fast spreading Internet-of-Things botnet that enslaved millions of devices for use in a series of massive distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks over the past six months. KrebsOnSecurity publicly named the suspect in February 2026 after the accused launched a volley of DDoS, doxing and swatting campaigns against this author and a security researcher. He now faces criminal hacking charges in both Canada and the United States.

items.title: CISA Enhances Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog to Include New Nomination Form
CISA Admin Leaked AWS GovCloud Keys on Github
items.description: Until this past weekend, a contractor for the Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) maintained a public GitHub repository that exposed credentials to several highly privileged AWS GovCloud accounts and a large number of internal CISA systems. Security experts said the public archive included files detailing how CISA builds, tests and deploys software internally, and that it represents one of the most egregious government data leaks in recent history.

items.title: Patch Tuesday, May 2026 Edition
items.description: Artificial intelligence platforms may be just as susceptible to social engineering as human beings, but they are proving remarkably good at finding security vulnerabilities in human-made computer code. That reality is on full display this month with some of the more widely-used software makers -- including Apple, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla and Oracle -- fixing near record volumes of security bugs, and/or quickening the tempo of their patch releases.

items.title: Canvas Breach Disrupts Schools & Colleges Nationwide
items.description: An ongoing data extortion attack targeting the widely-used education technology platform Canvas disrupted classes and coursework at school districts and universities across the United States today, after a cybercrime group defaced the service's login page with a ransom demand that threatened to leak data from 275 million students and faculty across nearly 9,000 educational institutions.

items.title: CISA Unveils New Initiative to Fortify America’s Critical Infrastructure
CISA, US and International Partners Release Guide to Secure Adoption of Agentic AI
Anti-DDoS Firm Heaped Attacks on Brazilian ISPs
items.description: A Brazilian tech firm that specializes in protecting networks from distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks has been enabling a botnet responsible for an extended campaign of massive DDoS attacks against other network operators in Brazil, KrebsOnSecurity has learned. The firm's chief executive says the malicious activity resulted from a security breach and was likely the work of a competitor trying to tarnish his company's public image.

items.title: CISA and U.S. Government Partners Unveil Guide to Accelerate Zero Trust Adoption in Operational Technology
CISA, National Cyber Security Centre (NCSC) UK, and Global Partners Issue Advisory on Chinese Government-Linked Covert Cyber Networks
CISA Warns of FIRESTARTER Malware Targeting Cisco ASA including Firepower and Secure Firewall Products
Student Loan Breach Exposes 2.5M Records
items.description: 2.5 million people were affected, in a breach that could spell more trouble down the line.

items.title: Watering Hole Attacks Push ScanBox Keylogger
items.description: Researchers uncover a watering hole attack likely carried out by APT TA423, which attempts to plant the ScanBox JavaScript-based reconnaissance tool.

items.title: Tentacles of ‘0ktapus’ Threat Group Victimize 130 Firms
items.description: Over 130 companies tangled in sprawling phishing campaign that spoofed a multi-factor authentication system.

items.title: Ransomware Attacks are on the Rise
items.description: Lockbit is by far this summer’s most prolific ransomware group, trailed by two offshoots of the Conti group.

items.title: Cybercriminals Are Selling Access to Chinese Surveillance Cameras
items.description: Tens of thousands of cameras have failed to patch a critical, 11-month-old CVE, leaving thousands of organizations exposed.

items.title: Twitter Whistleblower Complaint: The TL;DR Version
items.description: Twitter is blasted for security and privacy lapses by the company’s former head of security who alleges the social media giant’s actions amount to a national security risk.

items.title: Firewall Bug Under Active Attack Triggers CISA Warning
items.description: CISA is warning that Palo Alto Networks’ PAN-OS is under active attack and needs to be patched ASAP.

items.title: Fake Reservation Links Prey on Weary Travelers
items.description: Fake travel reservations are exacting more pain from the travel weary, already dealing with the misery of canceled flights and overbooked hotels.

items.title: iPhone Users Urged to Update to Patch 2 Zero-Days
items.description: Separate fixes to macOS and iOS patch respective flaws in the kernel and WebKit that can allow threat actors to take over devices and are under attack.

items.title: Google Patches Chrome’s Fifth Zero-Day of the Year
items.description: An insufficient validation input flaw, one of 11 patched in an update this week, could allow for arbitrary code execution and is under active attack.
